summary: Jeff Atwood challenges a Slashdot poster's claim that desktop computers consume 450 watts of power, demonstrating through actual measurements that even a high-end PC uses only around 132 watts at peak. Using a $30 Kill-a-Watt meter, he measures his own beefy workstation under various conditions and breaks down power consumption by component. The post serves as a broader critique of how people spread misinformation based on guesses rather than data, advocating for empirical measurement over estimation whenever possible.
key_points: A Slashdot poster's claim of 450W power consumption for desktop PCs is wildly exaggerated - actual measured peak usage is around 132W for a high-end system
Simple, inexpensive tools like a $30 Kill-a-Watt meter can provide concrete data that debunks common misconceptions
Power supply efficiency matters - about 25% of wall power is lost in conversion, so wall measurements overstate actual PC consumption
Even rough estimates become far more valuable when backed by actual supporting data
The tech community often perpetuates misinformation about hardware requirements (like needing 500W+ power supplies) that simple measurement could disprove
money_quotes: Why spread guesstimated misinformation when you can simply measure the power usage with a widely available $30 device?
Estimating is only necessary when you can't easily measure.
When you meet people like this, gently urge them to stop estimating and start measuring.
It's amazing how much you can infer from such simple, basic data collection.
As estimates go, these are backed by supporting data. And that's a lot more useful than the unsubstantiated wild guessing of random Slashdot posters.
I don't think you can realistically build a desktop computer that uses 450 watts!
key_insight: Measurement with simple tools beats speculation every time - stop guessing about things you can easily measure.
